title: Designing AI Features People Can Actually Use
objective: Translate model capability into trustworthy, fast, user-facing product behavior.
beginnerExplanation: AI product engineering is where model performance meets user experience. You decide when to stream, when to ask follow-up questions, how to show uncertainty, and how to recover when the system fails.
deeperExplanation: The best AI products feel narrow, intentional, and well-instrumented. They do not expose raw model capability everywhere. They wrap it in interaction design, telemetry, and feedback systems that let the team learn from real usage instead of assumptions.
keyTakeaways: AI UX should make uncertainty visible without making the product feel broken.
Latency, cost, and output quality are product variables, not just engineering variables.
Feedback loops are part of the feature, not an afterthought.
example: A writing assistant highlights citation confidence, lets the user accept or reject edits, and logs each correction so the team can see what the model keeps getting wrong.
commonMistakes: Adding AI to a surface without a clear user need.
Hiding uncertainty until a failure becomes obvious.
Launching without measuring real user task success.
exercise: Take an everyday workflow and sketch an AI feature that reduces time-to-value without turning the interface into a chat toy.
